serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pancit palabok weighs approximately 300g, comprising roughly 120g thin rice noodles, 80g annatto-shrimp sauce, and 100g mixed garnishes (chicharon, boiled shrimp, smoked fish flakes, hard-boiled egg, spring onion, calamansi).

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines noodle dishes) supplemented with USDA ARS SR Legacy for shrimp, egg, and component ingredient values
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Pancit palabok as a composite dish was constructed from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines noodle dish entries and USDA SR Legacy component values for shrimp, egg, and pork rinds. Per-100g values represent the weighted average of the assembled dish at the stated portion composition (120g noodles + 80g sauce + 100g garnishes per 300g plate). Values are consistent with the input record which cites the same approved sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Noodles and Mixed Noodle Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pancit palabok as a composite dish. GI of 65 is estimated from the primary carbohydrate component: thin rice noodles (bihon) with a published GI of approximately 61 (University of Sydney GI Database, rice vermicelli). The cornstarch-thickened annatto sauce raises the effective GI slightly above the noodle baseline. Protein from shrimp and egg moderates postprandial response. Estimated GI of 65 (medium range) is defensible and consistent with published rice noodle GI data. GL per 300g plate = GI × available carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 65 × 66 / 100 = 42.9 (very high).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1,560mg per 300g plate; 520mg per 100g) from chicharon, tinapa (smoked fish), shrimp sauce, and fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Pancit palabok is a high-sodium dish (approximately 1,560mg sodium per standard plate, representing 68–104% of recommended daily sodium intake); hypertensive patients should treat this as a special-occasion food only, and may reduce sodium burden by omitting or halving chicharon, requesting sauce on the side, and using calamansi juice in place of additional patis (fish sauce).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (GL ~43 per 300g plate) driven by 66g available carbohydrate from thin rice noodles and cornstarch-thickened sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pancit palabok carries a very high glycemic load of approximately 43 per standard plate, making it unsuitable for regular consumption by people with Type 2 Diabetes; portion reduction to approximately 80g noodles and omission of chicharon can lower GL to approximately 28, which remains high but more manageable when paired with additional protein and non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (1,560mg/plate), moderate cholesterol (~195mg/plate from shrimp and egg), and saturated fat from chicharon (fried pork rinds)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, pancit palabok should be consumed occasionally rather than regularly due to its high sodium load and moderate cholesterol content; reducing or omitting chicharon lowers saturated fat and sodium substantially, while the shrimp component provides modest omega-3 fatty acids and taurine with mild cardioprotective properties.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium may contribute to fluid retention; shrimp provides modest omega-3 and taurine with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may enjoy pancit palabok occasionally; the shrimp component provides taurine and modest omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefit, but the high sodium content (~1,560mg/plate) warrants caution due to potential fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pancit Palabok is a festive Filipino rice noodle dish consisting of thin rice vermicelli (bihon) topped with a thick, vivid orange annatto-shrimp gravy sauce and an elaborate array of garnishes including fried pork rinds (chicharon), boiled shrimp, smoked fish flakes (tinapa), sliced hard-boiled egg, chopped spring onion, and fresh calamansi. The dish is a centrepiece of Filipino celebrations — birthdays, fiestas, and parties — where the long noodles symbolise long life in Filipino-Chinese cultural tradition. The annatto (atsuete) seeds give the sauce its characteristic deep orange colour through the natural carotenoid pigment bixin, with no artificial colouring. Nutritionally, a standard 300g plate provides approximately 525 kcal, 27g protein, 18g fat, and 66g available carbohydrate, with a very high glycemic load of approximately 43 and a high sodium content of approximately 1,560mg. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free (rice noodles) but contains shellfish, fish, pork, and egg allergens. Clinically, it is a high-GL, high-sodium dish appropriate for special occasions rather than regular consumption for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The shrimp component contributes taurine and modest omega-3 fatty acids. Pancit palabok is widely available at Filipino restaurants in Singapore (notably Lucky Plaza, Orchard Road) and at Filipino community catering events.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines noodle dishes) and USDA ARS SR Legacy for component ingredients. Both are approved primary sources. Per-100g values are internally consistent with the stated 300g plate composition and the described macronutrient profile (175 kcal/100g × 3 = 525 kcal/plate; 9g protein × 3 = 27g; 6g fat × 3 = 18g; 22g carb × 3 = 66g). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli data (GI ~61) with defensible upward adjustment for cornstarch sauce component. Confidence rated high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for pancit palabok as a composite dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require full recipe disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled dish is not reported in the cited sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this dish composition — no hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation). Glycemic load per 300g plate = 65 × 66 / 100 = 42.9 (very high GL). Annatto (atsuete) provides natural bixin carotenoid colouring — no artificial colouring. Shrimp cholesterol approximately 195mg per 300g plate (65mg/100g × 3). This is a Filipino fiesta and celebration dish; long noodles carry cultural symbolism of long life. Halal status is false due to pork chicharon content. Dish is gluten-free (rice noodles, cornstarch) and dairy-free. FAO/INFOODS SEA is an approved primary source for Southeast Asian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
